We also recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan draft guidelines to be followed in the payment of financial assistance to ensure proper identification of beneficiaries and appropriate procedure in the grant and disbursement of funds.
observations.reason: Not Implemented

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the financial reports on the operations of the local economic enterprises and for the department heads to adopt the existing manual in the planning, budgeting, implementation and reporting of the LEEs.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the Sangguniang Bayan conduct periodic review of the operations of LEEs and evaluate the profitability and self-sufficiency of the individual LEEs. Amendments of Revenue Code involving collections of fees by LEEs for a more viable and profitable operations may be considered, notwithstanding the services provided therein.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to record all transfers/advances as Receivables in the books of GF - Proper of the Municipality and Liability in the books of the MEEDO. Correcting entries should be made for the transfer of fund amounting to ₱3,500,000.00 from GF to MEEDO for fair presentation of financial statements. 

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant apply the income generated by the MEEDO for the return of the advances made from the GF in accordance with the provisions of Section 313 of RA 7160 and 3.2 of LBC 100.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to send out statement of accounts monthly to market stallholders reminding them of their monthly rentals to improve collection efficiency.

We further recommended that the OIC-Municipal Treasurer send demand letters/ Notice of non-payment to tenants/lessee of market stalls with unpaid accounts/adhere to the provisions as stipulated in the Revenue Code of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements monthly and submit the same to the Auditor for verification pursuant to Section 74 of PD 1445.  Timely preparation and verification of the financial reports will guide management in making sound decisions regarding the financial matters of the Municipality. 

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant enroll in the Internet Banking Facility for Institutional Clients of the Land Bank of the Philippines (weAccess) to secure updated bank statements.
observations.managementAction: There is delay in submission of BRS due to delay in the issuance of the bank statements

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:

Reduce the number of Job Hires especially those performing clerical and administrative functions. Hiring of workers should be made as actual need arises and be deployed only to offices that needed their services the most. 

Require the rendition of overtime services by regular employees in the exigency of the service and to meet reports on deadlines instead of hiring job hires. Adopt merit and incentive award strategies to motivate all personnel and to enhance their potentials in performing their functions. Institute regular job rotation to place the right person on the right job to deliver the services required.

Revisit the guidelines set forth in CSC MC No. 24, s. 2002 on the hiring of job hires and contractual personnel.  Judicious use of government funds must be considered so that savings derived could be used on developmental projects most beneficial in uplifting the lives of the constituents.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive stop the grant of Hazard Pay to PSW and PSWDW who are not actually exposed to hazard or occupational risk, strictly observe the provisions of RA 9433 and its IRR and direct the:

Municipal Welfare Development Officer to submit the:

Approved list of positions in its plantilla with corresponding job description/actual duties that describe the basis for justifying such positions that are exposed to occupational risks and assigned to places stated in Item 16.1 of the IRR;

ii.   Established procedure for identifying personnel who shall be exposed to hazardous areas and occupational risks as stated in Item 16.1.1, including the duration of such exposure; and

PSW and PSWDW who claimed Hazard Pay to submit actual accomplishment showing actual exposure to hazard and occupational risk on the period they claimed, otherwise, the amount of ₱ 138,419.00 will be properly returned by the payees.
observations.managementAction:
Documents were already submitted.

ND will be issued for the amount claimed totaling ₱138,419.00.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Tourism Officer in charge of the activity cause the submission of the liquidation reports for each tribe to establish the validity of disbursements.

We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive issue a Memorandum of Agreement to the different tribes prior to the release of funds. The requisites in granting the assistance, liquidation terms and accomplishment reports should be clearly stated in the Memorandum of Agreement. Issuance of Official Receipts should also be complied with, to ensure proper accounting of funds received.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to complete the physical inventory taking and submit the RPCPPE by type/class of property, indicating in the Remarks column the persons accountable therefore, the location and/or whereabouts of the properties. Verify from the accounting records the list of PPE recorded in the books and account for the difference.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the balances of PPE per books against the RPCPPE prepared by the Inventory Committee and any difference shall be accounted for in order that the PPE accounts reflected in the financial statements are fairly presented.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Head, BAC Secretariat and Municipal Accountant to attach the documents required for procurement through public bidding pursuant to Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate at least 1% of Internal Revenue Allotment intended for the implementation of PPAs of LCPC, for endorsement to the Sangguniang Bayan in accordance with Section 15 of RA 9344 and DILG Circular No, 2012-120.

We further recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator include in the Annual Investment Plan the PPAs for the LCPC intended for the best interests of children.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor regulate the utilization of financial assistance and ensure that expenses intended for socio-cultural activities be expended pursuant to the provisions of the Section 4(2) of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-003 dated October 29, 2012.

We also recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan draft guidelines to be followed in the payment of financial assistance to ensure proper identification of beneficiaries and appropriate procedure in the grant and disbursement of funds.

We recommended that the agency strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 325(a) of RA No. 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98, dated October 14, 2011 so as to avoid an imbalance in the allocation of funds favoring personal services and as to not reduce the funds available for the delivery of basic services and development projects.

We also recommended the refund of the disallowed Productivity Enhancement Incentive in the total amount of ₱2,545,723.00.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to complete the physical inventory taking and submit the RPCPPE by type/class of property, indicating in the Remarks column the persons accountable therefore, the location and/or whereabouts of the properties. Verify from the accounting records the list of PPE recorded in the books and account for the difference.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-01 dated February 4, 2019.
Management Comment
The Municipal Accountant commented that they have difficulty in conducting the physical count of inventory and ascertaining the value of the PPEs because there was no turn-over of documents such as ledger cards by the previous administration.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The audit team maintained that the inventory committee should begin with current purchases then work back on previous years properties. Likewise, each department/office should conduct inventory of PPEs assigned to them. Further, it is suggested that the GSO/inventory committee submit the initial Report of Physical Count of Property Plant and Equipment in the prescribed format, with the properties that are available and serviceable and the committee to exert effort to locate later, those properties that cannot be reconciled with accounting records.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to deposit her collections intact or in full amount as received daily, or on the next banking day to the authorized depository bank and to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 69 of PD No. 1445 and Section 32 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer reconcile the accounting and cashbook balances every end of the month and that of prior years’ adjustment of ₱257,417.13.
We likewise recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer monitor and require the Market Collectors to remit their collections daily and submit explanation on the difference between the cashbook balances and cash counted on January 9, 2018 amounting to ₱18,393.67.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-03 dated February 4, 2019.
Management Comments
The Acting Municipal Treasurer commented that reminders were given to all collectors, market deputized collectors, Alternate Liquidating Officer, and Liquidating Officer.
The Municipal Mayor resolved to issue a memorandum with warning to market collectors with regards to non-remittance of collections daily and to create a system which includes sanctions for repeated violations.
The Municipal Accountant commented that there is a provision in the Local Government Code for the daily remittance and that such non-remittance of collections daily is only acceptable when the distance is remote.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The audit team noted and commended the actions taken by management.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to regularly monitor transactions affecting reciprocal accounts to ensure that all transactions are accurately recorded in the books.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to trace up the unreconciled balances of Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds amounting to ₱9,542,087.74 and to make necessary adjustments, where appropriate, to ensure fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-02 dated February 4, 2019.
Management Comment
The Municipal Accountant commented that correcting entries were made and the accounts were now reconciled.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct:
The officials and employees concerned to liquidate or refund immediately the cash advances granted to them.
The Municipal Accountant to send demand letters to the concerned officials and employees.
The HRMO to cause the suspension of payment of the concerned employees’ salaries until they have fully liquidate their cash advances.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-05 dated February 8, 2019.
Management Comments
The Municipal Accountant commented that granting of cash advances to the liquidating officer was stopped since it was observed that most of the unliquidated cash advances were under his name and due to the bulk of transactions, liquidations could not be made. Further, the immense amount of unliquidated cash advances was due to lack of manpower in the treasurer’s office and since most project implementors prefer the same liquidating officer to pay for wages.
The Municipal Accountant also informed the audit team that demand letters will be issued for the unliquidated cash advances for travels.
The Municipal Mayor resolved to issue a memorandum directing all department heads to prioritize the liquidation of their outstanding cash advances and such unliquidated cash advances be reduced significantly for CY 2019. The Municipal Mayor also directed the administrator to create a team dedicated only for the purpose of overseeing the liquidation of cash advances and inform those persons with unliquidated cash advances, to liquidate the same.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The audit team maintained that before granting cash advances, the Municipal Accountant should make sure that the previous cash advances were already liquidated since, per schedule, the transactions were the same. Likewise, the non -liquidation of cash advances is justifiable only for the current year, and the prior years’ cash advances should have been liquidated. The audit team also requested from the Municipal Mayor a copy of memorandum to be issued to employees and all directives, as part of the actions taken by management.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer to monitor the implementation and thoroughly inspect the completion of projects to determine whether or not there were justifiable reasons for the delays incurred.
We likewise recommended that the Municipal Accountant compute the damages and deduct the same before processing the final claims of the contractor.
We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor explain in writing the reason for non-imposition of appropriate sanctions over and above the LD paid by contractor RB Alcazar Construction Corporation.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-15 dated February 17, 2019.
Management Comments
The Municipal Accountant commented that sometime during June or July of 2018, they were about to rescind the contract, however, they estimated that the project would delay even longer if re-contracting will be made. Also one of the reason that they did not rescind the contract was that the company already delivered the materials and with the intention of completing the project. They estimated also that they would lose more with re-contracting especially if the purpose of the project is for supplying/delivering water to the constituents during El Niño.
The Municipal Mayor commented that they will blacklist the contractor from participating in the Municipality of Lambunao.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The audit team leader suggested that they should document/record the work history of the contractor as basis of BAC if the same contractor tries to participate in the procurement process in the future.
The audit team maintained that, at the Municipal Mayor’s level, they should submit a written explanation for their decision of not imposing appropriate sanctions over and above the LD paid by the contractor.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate at least 1% of Internal Revenue Allotment intended for the implementation of PPAs of LCPC, for endorsement to the Sangguniang Bayan in accordance with Section 15 of RA 9344 and DILG Circular No, 2012-120.
We further recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator include in the Annual Investment Plan the PPAs for the LCPC intended for the best interests of children.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-06 dated February 8, 2019.
Management Comments
The Municipal Budget Officer commented that although no allocation for PPAs of LCPC was made in the Annual Budget of CY 2018, such PPAs were attributed from different offices which was more than the 1% mandate of RA No. 9344.
The Municipal Mayor discussed that the Municipality of Lambunao was identified and awarded as one of the most child-friendly municipalities due to its programs for such cause. The Municipal Mayor also directed the Municipal Budget Officer that PPAs for LCPC should now be allocated and not attributed.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The audit team maintained that allocation should be made and not attribution for the PPAs of LCPC. The audit team also noted that allocation for 1% IRA for LCPC should be done in CY 2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and Local School Board prepare the budget for Special Education Fund in accordance with the rules and regulations of RA 5447 so that funds will be spent for the intended purpose as advanced in the regulation.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to see to it that all disbursement vouchers are duly supported with complete documentation to ensure the propriety, validity and legality of the claims and to avoid suspension of disbursements in audit.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-07 dated February 6, 2019.
Management Comments
The Municipal Accountant commented that LNHS temporarily rented the classrooms of Yamato International School just to accommodate the numerous quantity of enrollees because LNHS building is being constructed.
The Municipal Accountant commented that they will comply with the audit team’s recommendation on the procurement of various purchases for the Special Education Fund since, although the fund is being utilized for the schools, the implementing agency is still the municipality hence, should therefore undergo the procurement process.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
We maintained that procurement charged to SEF should pass thru the BAC and abide with the IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local Finance Committee and the Local Sanggunian to observe the provisions of DILG-DBM JC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 on the use of 20% Development Fund, so that proper utilization and implementation of the developmental projects will be carried out effectively and efficiently.
We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant stop the practice of charging regular operating expenses against the 20% Development Fund and to charge the same instead to MOOE and/or Capital Outlay of the General Fund. Henceforth, future similar charges will be disallowed in audit.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-08 dated February 8, 2019.
Management Comments
The Municipal Accountant commented, with regards to soil stabilizers, that no study was made to prove that soil stabilization will last for only a year.
The Municipal Mayor commented that soil stabilization is intended for specific areas that are in place of concreting and that soil stabilizer is expected to last longer than five years and shall remain permanent as stated in the brochure.
The Municipal Engineer commented that soil stabilizer is good if vehicles passing through the road are light, but based on his observation at Barangay Cali, more or less after three months of completion of soil stabilized road, potholes appeared when 10-wheeler or six-wheeler trucks pass through and it was immediately layered with concrete without applying base course since the road is already stable.
The Municipal Mayor refuted that the potholes appeared not because of the weight of vehicles passing through but rather the composition of the soil and proportion of chemicals.
With regards to the disbursement for dewormer, the Municipal Accountant commented that there was an error in charging and issuance of check since disbursement for dewormer was not part of 20% DF.
With regards to the repair and rehab of Brgy Cunarom irrigation water entrance, the Municipal Mayor directed the Municipal Engineer to prepare an inspection report since this was destroyed by the quarrying company and that the quarrying company should be responsible for the repair.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The audit team commented that the Municipal Accountant should assess whether or not the soil stabilizer may be recorded as capital investment.
The audit team also suggested that monitoring should be done on the effectiveness of soil stabilizer on sitio Cali and Barangay Cunarom.
With regards to small farm reservoir, the audit team maintained that small farm reservoir with immaterial amount should not be charged to 20% development fund

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the LDRRM Officer to assess the adequacy and availability of basic rescue and response equipment in the LDRRM Office  and to prioritize the procurement of early warning systems, search, rescue and retrieval equipment and other equipage useful for disaster preparedness and disaster response.
We further recommended that the LDRRM Officer revisit DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-73 on the utilization of Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund (LDRRMF).
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-11 dated February 13, 2019.
Management Comments
The DRRM Officer commented that the construction of DRRM storage was included by the previous LDRRM head but this year 2019, they are basing their charges from the AIP which includes the four thematic areas.
With regards to the catering services, the LDRRM Officer commented that the program is Response Caravan by the Regional Disaster Risk Reduction Management.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The audit team maintained that LDRRM stockroom is administrative in nature and the payment for the construction should be charged to the general fund.
The audit team likewise suggested that management should make sure that there is training on preparedness when charging catering services to LDRRM Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRMO to:
Prepare a LDRRMFIP with the details of the funded projects and activities for disaster Prevention and Mitigation, Preparedness, Response and Rehabilitation and Recovery in the DRRM Investment Plan pursuant to 5.1.2 COA Circular No. 2012-02 dated September 12, 2012
Submit a monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF duly certified by the Municipal Accountant to the auditor for review and verification
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-11 dated February 13, 2019.
Management Comment
Management made no comment on the observation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require:
The MEEDO Head to revisit the ordinances relative to revenue generations particularly on the operation of the Municipal Economic Enterprise to ensure that the provisions of the revenue code were efficiently and effectively implemented;
The MEEDO Head to submit monthly reports on the status of receipts and collections including statement of delinquencies of the economic enterprises;
The Municipal Treasurer to formulate strategic measures to intensify collection efficiency especially in RGU Lambunao Water System.
The Local Finance Committee, the MEEDO Head and the Sangguniang Bayan to conduct an assessment of all existing RGUs in order to determine and resolve the incurrence of loss.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider going over the manual on procedures, if financial self-sufficiency is not achieved, i.e. item 7.3(e) of LBC No. 111.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-09 dated February 8, 2019.
Management Comments
The Municipal Budget Officer commented that some non-income generating RGUs were transferred to the General Fund in CY 2019 such as the Motorpool, and Tourism.
The MEEDO Head commented that the slaughterhouse started operating only in CY 2016 though it was included in the Special Ordinance No. 10-001 and the Lambunao Water System started its full operation only in CY 2017.
She also mentioned that with regards to the PS cost of MEEDO, together with the HR are working on the structure of personnel for the MEEDO. Some personnel charged therein were transferred back to the general fund.
She also mentioned that there is a pending request to the SB on the upgrading of fees and charges for the services provided by the MEEDO.
The Municipal Accountant commented that the accounting booked the individual RGUs separately, however no separate financial statements were made due to lack of manpower.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The audit team maintained that there should be a separate financial statement for each RGU to determine their profitability and it is noted that the accounting has made separate financial statements for each RGUs effective CY 2019. Likewise, the accounting should coordinate and reconcile its reports with MEEDO Head.
Also, the personnel that are not functioning under the MEEDO should not be charged to the MEEDO Fund.
With regards to the Lambunao Water System, the income earned in CY 2018 should have been recorded in the books since the accounting follows the accrual basis which means that income and expenses are recorded at the time they are earned and incurred. Likewise, the receivable should have been reflected in the financial statements.
We commended the actions taken by the MEEDO Head however, they should follow up their pending request to the SB since it could augment the income of MEEDO.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor discontinue the practice of authorizing the attendance of Job Hires to trainings and seminars and to claim travelling expenses and per diems to prevent incurrence of additional maintenance and other operating expenses.
We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the officials concerned to assess the importance/necessity of trainings and seminars and that only permanent employees will be allowed to attend the same so that knowledge gain out of it will be useful in the performance of actual work.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-10 dated February 12, 2019.
Management Comments
The Municipal Mayor commented that they will discontinue this practice, however with regards to some Job Hires that are listed or under the Mayor’s Office, some of them are now hired as regular employees and some are in the process of regularization of their employment with the municipality. He also commented that some of them are already pipelined since last year.
The Municipal Mayor added that those Job Hires that were pipelined and authorized to travel were the ones handling liaison with Jollibee Foods Corporation as partners with Lambunao. They are handling the program “Farmers Entrepreneurship Program” which is a year-long relationship to conclude this year 2019, and its purpose is for the municipality and the farmers to supply vegetables to its subsidiary, the Chowking.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The audit team maintained that those Job Hires that are temporary and not in the process of regularization should not be allowed/authorized to travel since they have no employee-employer relationship with the municipality. Also, their travelling expenses will be taken up in the travelling budget that are alloted for the regular employees especially that a new executive order for travel was issued.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Head, BAC Secretariat and Municipal Accountant to attach the documents required for procurement through public bidding pursuant to Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-12 dated February 14, 2019.
Management Comments
The BAC chairman pledged to comply the lacking supporting documents according to the requirements.
The BAC secretariat mentioned that she has already requested for a replacement from the mayor.
The Municipal Mayor resolved to change the organization structure of the BAC and directed the BAC chairman to propose or recommend willing employees to reconstitute the BAC membership starting May 2019.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The audit team maintained that the requirements should have already been complied at the post qualification evaluation process of the BAC. The audit team also suggested that they should assign a willing employee to function as BAC secretariat since the present BAC secretariat also functions as staff of GSO which has heavy workload.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the BAC and the BAC secretariat to strictly comply with the 2016 Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA No. 9184 and its amendments to ensure that all procurement awards and decisions will result in the most advantageous price and terms for the municipality.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-12 dated February 14, 2019.
Management Comment
The BAC Chairman admitted that the error was due to misconstrued provision of the IRR, but promised to comply with all recommendations based on the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of RA No. 9184 and its amendments.
(a) The propriety and reasonableness of the prices of the contracts entered into by the municipality were not immediately ascertained because copy of perfected contracts and purchase orders were not submitted to the Auditor within five (5) working days from execution and issuance together with the supporting documents, contrary to Section 3.1 of COA Circular No. 2009 – 001, thus prevented the auditor from conducting a timely review and evaluation of the procurement.
(b) Contracts and Memorandum of Agreements were not notarized, thus said documents could not be considered as public documents and are not admissible in court as evidence.
COA Circular No. 2009-001, dated February 12, 2009 states that:
Xxx
Coverage –
This circular shall cover all contracts , purchase orders, and the like, entered into    by any government agency irrespective of amount involved.
Xxx
Submission and Review Process –
Contracts
Within five (5) working days from the execution of a contract by the government or any of its subdivisions, agencies or instrumentalities, including government-owned and controlled corporations and their subsidiaries, a copy of said contract and each of all the documents forming part thereof by reference or incorporation shall be furnished to the Auditor of the agency concerned. x xx
Purchase Orders
A copy of any purchase order irrespective of amount, and each and every supporting document, shall, within five (5) working days from issuance thereof, be submitted to the Auditor concerned. x xx.
xxxxx
Penalty Clause
4.1   Any unjustified failure of the officials and employees concerned to comply with the requirements herein imposed shall be subject to the administrative disciplinary action provided in (a) Section 127 of Presidential Decree No. 1445; (b) Section 55, Title I-B, Book V of the Revised Administrative Code of 1987; and (c) Section 11 of Republic Act No. 6713.”
Moreover, Section 30, Rule 132, Rules of Court provides:
Section 30. Proof of notarial documents. – Every instrument duly acknowledged or proved and certified as provided by law, may be presented in evidence without further proof, the certificate of acknowledgment being prima facie evidence of the execution of the instrument or document involved.
The act of notarization gives the presumption that the parties executed the document. It is not a mere meaningless ritual but of great importance as it provides sufficient evidence in court without proof of its due execution.
Post audit of transactions for the period January 1 to December, 2018  revealed that the municipality failed to submit copies of contracts and purchase orders to the Auditor within five (5) working days from its execution and issuance. In addition, it was observed that some of the needed data in the purchase orders were not indicated, to wit:
Purchase Order number
Mode of procurement
Date of delivery
Payment term
Complete item name and complete item specifications
Paragraph providing penalty on late delivery
Failure to submit contracts and purchase orders in a timely manner for review, precluded the auditor from ascertaining the reasonableness of the prices at the time of purchase and the proprietary of the contracts entered into by the municipality.  Likewise, defects and deficiencies noted could not be immediately brought to the attention of the municipality for appropriate corrective measures.
Post audit also revealed that contracts and Memorandum of Agreements entered into by the municipality for various infrastructure projects, programs and activities undertaken through public bidding were not notarized thus could not be considered as public instrument that are admissible in court as evidence.
Recommendations
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the General Service Officer to submit to the Auditor a copy of all contracts, MOAs, purchase orders and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof for reference or incorporation therein within five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts, MOAs and issuance of purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001, so that review, inspection and evaluation could be made accordingly.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the GSO that all service contract, job order contract, MOAs and all contracts involving infrastructure projects, programs and activities undertaken through public bidding be notarized to properly safeguard the interest of the municipality.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-04 dated February 6, 2019.
Management Comments
The Municipal Accountant commented that the provision for late delivery were already applied or included in the contracts and purchase orders.
The Municipal Mayor resolved to comply with the recommendations and that all future contracts and POs would include provision for late delivery.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The audit team maintained that the requirements should have already been complied at the post qualification evaluation process of the BAC. The audit team also suggested that they should assign a willing employee to function as BAC secretariat since the present BAC secretariat also functions as staff of GSO which has heavy workload.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and officials concerned to stop the incurrence of expenses that are religious in nature and to strictly adhere to the guidelines prescribed by the above cited-laws, rules and regulations.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and officials concerned cause the refund of the total amount of ₱120,916.00 spent for fiesta celebration and cash prizes for Karera sa Lambunao.
The aforementioned audit observation was communicated to management thru Audit Observation Memorandum No.2019-16 and No. 2019-17 dated February 18, 2019.
Management Comment
The Municipal Accountant commented that the payment for fireworks display was taken from the proceeds of the fiesta (Trust Fund).
The Municipal Accountant commented that the Karera sa Lambunao was a community event that is being handled by the church that is counter-parted by the LGU, but the activity is not religious.
Auditor’s Rejoinder
The audit team replied that the payment for the fireworks display should be supported with documents.
The audit team maintained that the disbursement was supported with a letter-request from the parish priest in celebration of the patronal fiesta. Likewise, the payment of cash prize is being discouraged and will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Disposal Committee to follow the disposal procedures and accomplish the forms to be used in reporting disposable supplies and property.

We likewise recommended that the GSO-designate dispose all unserviceable properties beyond repair and value less records according to the guidelines on Disposal.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to deposit all undeposited collections to the authorized depository bank.


We further recommended that henceforth, collections be deposited intact and daily and to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 69 of PD 1445 and Section 32 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and OIC-Municipal Treasurer reconcile the accounting and cashbook balances every end of the month and prior years’ adjustment of ₱622,781.77.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the OIC-Municipal Treasurer to submit explanation on the difference between the cashbook balances and cash counted on January 12, 2018 amounting to ₱528,303.51

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to regularly monitor transactions affecting reciprocal account to ensure that all transactions are accurately recorded in the books of accounts.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant conduct a reconciliation between the reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds regularly and to account for the difference amounting to ₱1,761,191.20 for a fair presentation of the accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct officials and employees concerned to liquidate or refund immediately the cash advances granted them.  Demand letters should be sent to the concerned officials and employees.  If remained unheeded, the Local Chief Executive may cause or order the suspension of payment of their salaries until they have fully liquidated thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

Require all municipal officials and employees concerned to settle their audit suspension and disallowances in the total amount of ₱6,985,124.27 pursuant to Section 5.4 of the RRSA. 

Instruct the Municipal Accountant to record in the books of accounts the balance of Receivables – Disallowance and Charges as of December 31, 2017 in the total amount of ₱6,160,161.14.

Ensure that all municipal officials and employees strictly comply with the rules and regulations on the settlement of accounts and to monitor such compliance.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local Finance Committee and the Local Sanggunian to observe the provisions of DILG-DBM JC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 on the use of 20% Development Fund, so that proper utilization and implementation of the developmental projects will be carried out effectively and efficiently.

We recommended that Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant stop the practice of charging regular operating expenses to 20% Development Fund and to charge the same instead to MOOE and/or Capital Outlay. Henceforth, succeeding changes will not be allowed in audit.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to follow the guidelines prescribed in DILG-DBM JC No. 2017-1, to ensure coordination of activities and proper implementation and utilization of 20% Development Fund by aligning expenditures with identified activities in the approved Annual Investment Plan.

Procurement procedures as mandated under Republic Act 9184 should be strictly followed.
We further recommended that the Municipal Treasurer set up petty cash fund for payment of petty operating expenses.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee instruct BAC Secretariat to post all procurement in the Portal as prescribed in the above-mentioned laws, rules and regulations to promote transparency and achieve efficiency in the procurement process of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) in all financial transactions of the municipality and attach the same to disbursement vouchers, liquidation reports, report of disbursements and collections and journals pursuant to Section 31 of the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units, Volume II.

We recommended that the General Service Officer submit to the auditor a copy of all contracts, purchase orders and the like, including all supporting documents forming part thereof by reference or incorporation within five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer enforce the implementation of Municipal Tax Ordinance No. 13-001 Series of 2013 through collection of SWM fees.
We further recommended that the Municipal Vice Mayor monitor the implementation of Municipal Tax Ordinance No. 13-001 Series of 2013, which is the collection of garbage fees.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should implement the provisions of COA Circular No. 96-011 and record adjustments for valid reconciling items as reflected in the BRS prepared by the Accounting Office. The BRS submitted to the COA Auditor should be supported by copies of the JEV recording reconciling items that require correction in the books of accounts, as well as a copy of the letter to the respective bank informing them of errors (if any) noted in the BRS. Prior to actual reconciliation, verification of supporting paid checks, debit memos and credit memos, shall be made, upon receipt of the bank statements,  to ensure that debits and credits made in the bank statements are properly documented by the bank.
We further recommended that the Accounting Office exert more effort in determining the unreconciled credit memo, and other reconciling items, and properly label the items in the Bank Reconciliation Statement. Cash balances and transactions should be monitored regularly to determine proper use of the accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and LDRRM Officer-Designate see to it that charges to LDRRMF are in accordance with NDRRMC, DBM and DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013 and COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant and MDRRM Officer-Designate prioritize the procurement of early warning systems, search, rescue and retrieval equipment and other equipage useful for disaster preparedness and disaster response other than administrative supplies and equipment.
We likewise recommended that the LDRRM Officer-Designate submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment charged to LDRRM Fund which include the newly procured items.
We recommend that henceforth, the MDRRM Officer-Designate submit the LDRRM Plan for CY 2016 and the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF. Projects and activities charged against the LDRRMF shall be incorporated in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan (LDRRMP), and integrated in the approved Annual Investment Program (AIP) of the LGU.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
1. Reduce the number of Job Hires especially those performing clerical and administrative functions. Hiring of workers should be made as actual need arises and be deployed only to offices that needed their services the most.
2. Require the rendition of overtime services by regular employees in the exigency of the service and to meet reports on deadlines instead of hiring job hires. Adopt merit and incentive award strategies to motivate all personnel and to enhance their potentials in performing their functions. Institute regular job rotation to place the right person on the right job to deliver the services required.
3. Revisit the guidelines set forth in CSC MC No. 24, s. 2002 on the hiring of job hires and contractual personnel.  Judicious use of government funds must be considered so that savings derived could be used on developmental projects most beneficial in uplifting the lives of the constituents.

We recommended that the Municipality of Lambunao through the Sangguniang Bayan hire qualified personnel to occupy the position of LDRRM Officer and three (3) Staff following the position titles and salary grades set by Rule 6 Section 2 of IRR of RA No. 10121 and Item 5.2, 5.3 and 5.4 of Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014 to be able to properly perform their functions and responsibilities as LDRRM Officer and Staff.
We recommended that the MDRRM Officer-Designate religiously observe the Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF (Annex B) to the COA Auditor copy furnished the OCD and DILG-LGOO.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and the Local School Board stop the practice of charging expenditures not within the purview of RA 5447 and the provisions of RA 7160. Future similar transactions contrary to above cited laws, rules and regulations will be accordingly disallowed in audit.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive:
1. Require the Lambunao Water District to comply with the provision of the MOA to share at least two percent (2%) of the total collection/income derived from the project.
2. Oversee the compliance of the second party to the provisions of the MOA executed by and between the Municipality of Lambunao and Lambunao Water District.
3. Require the officials concerned the submission of the MOA by and between the Municipality and the DILG to ascertain compliance of the provisions on the implementation of the SALINTUBIG Project.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Project In-Charge to monitor the projects implemented by the municipality especially those already turned over to barangays and require the recipient-barangay to comply with the provision of the MOA.
We also recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Punong Barangay, as the Recipient and the Project In-Charge to determine the solution to the problems being encountered in the non- operationalization of the water system.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive stop the grant of Hazard Pay to PSW and PSWDW who are not actually exposed to hazard or occupational risk, strictly observe the provisions of RA 9433 and its IRR and direct the:
1.  Municipal Welfare Development Officer to submit the:
i.   	Approved list of positions in its plantilla with corresponding job description/actual duties that describe the basis for justifying such positions that are exposed to occupational risks and assigned to places stated in Item 16.1 of the IRR;
ii.   Established procedure for identifying personnel who shall be exposed to hazardous areas and occupational risks as stated in Item 16.1.1, including the duration of such exposure; and
2. PSW and PSWDW who claimed Hazard Pay to submit actual accomplishment showing actual exposure to hazard and occupational risk on the period they claimed, otherwise, the amount of P 138,419.00 will be properly returned by the payees.

We recommended that the OIC-Municipal Treasurer deposit all undeposited collections to the authorized depository bank and to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 69 of PD 1445 and Section 32 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.
We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant and OIC-Municipal Treasurer reconcile the Cash in Treasury account balance as of December 31, 2016 with that of the OIC-Municipal Treasurer’s Cash book and any difference must be accounted for.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive stop the incurrence of expenses that are religious in nature pursuant to the provisions of Section 4(2) of PD 1445 and Section 335 of RA 7160, henceforth expenses of the same nature will not be allowed in audit.

We recommended that the BAC Chairman and the Municipal Accountant see to it that all required/necessary bidding documents were attached to disbursement vouchers for payment of purchases or contracts through public bidding pursuant to Section 9.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001, dated June 14, 2012.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive instruct the employees concerned to strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184 to ensure efficiency and economy in its procurement process. The alternative mode of procurement must be resorted to only in highly exceptional cases and whenever justified by the conditions provided for in the IRR of the same Act.
We further recommend that the amount of P180,000.00 paid for the acquisition of the SMS Alert System through Direct Contracting be refunded.
We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer deposit all undeposited collections to the authorized depository bank and to strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 69 of PD 1445 and Section 32 of MNGAS for LGU, Vol. 1.
We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the employees/officials concerned the immediate settlement of all cash advances after the purpose for which these were granted had been made thereof pursuant to Section 89 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002.
We recommend that the agency strictly adhere with the provisions of Section 325(a) of RA 7160 and Local Budget Circular No. 98, dated October 14, 2011 so as to avoid an imbalance in the allocation of funds favoring personal services and as to not reduce the funds available for the delivery of basic services and development projects.
We also recommend the refund of the disallowed Productivity Enhancement Incentive in the total amount of P2,545,723.00.
We recommend that the Local Chief Executive require the Local Finance Committee to advise the Local Sanggunian to observe the provisions of DILG-DBM, Joint Memorandum No.2011-1 on the use of 20% Development Fund, so that proper utilization and implementation of the developmental projects will be carried out effectively and efficiently.
We recommend that the municipality discontinue the payment of Office supplies, repair and improvement of District Office and equipment  for use of District Offices charged against the Special Education Fund.  Section 1 of RA 5447 on the prioritization of expenses chargeable to SEF must be strictly followed.
We also recommend that the Local School Board prepare the budget for Special Education Fund in accordance with the rules and regulations of RA 5447 so that funds will be spent for the intended purpose as advanced in the regulation.
We recommend that the Municipal Treasurer strictly adhere to the provisions of Section 68 of Presidential Decree No. 1445 so that transfer of funds will be properly accounted for. Furthermore, the Official Receipt issued will serve as proof of the occurrence of the transaction as well as to ensure accurate recording of the funds received.
	
We also recommend that the Municipal Treasurer make a correcting entry in her cashbook taking up the collections in the concerned funds affected.
We recommend that the Tourism Officer in charge of the activity cause the submission of the liquidation reports for each tribe to establish the validity of disbursements.
We also recommend that the Local Chief Executive issue a Memorandum of Agreement to the different tribes prior to the release of funds. The requisites in granting the assistance, liquidation terms and accomplishment reports should be clearly stated in the Memorandum of Agreement. Issuance of Official Receipts should also be complied with, to ensure proper accounting of funds received.
We recommend that the Local Chief Executive instruct the employees concerned to strictly adhere to the provisions of RA 9184 to ensure efficiency and economy in its procurement process. The alternative mode of procurement must be resorted to only in highly exceptional cases and whenever justified by the conditions provided for in the IRR of the same Act.
We further recommend that the amount of P180,000.00 paid for the acquisition of the SMS Alert System through Direct Contracting be refunded.
We recommend that the Municipal Accountant prepare the Bank Reconciliation Statements monthly and submit the same to the Auditor for verification pursuant to Section 74 of PD 1445.  Timely preparation and verification of the financial reports will guide management in making sound decisions regarding the financial matters of the Municipality. 

We recommend that the General Service Office submit to the Auditor a copy of all contracts, purchase orders and the like, and all supporting documents forming part thereof for reference or incorporation therein within five (5) working days from the execution of the contracts and issuance of purchase orders pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001, so that review, inspection and evaluation could be conducted accordingly.

We recommend that the Municipal DRRM Officer adhere strictly with the provision of Item 5.1.5 of COA Circular 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 and submit regularly to the COA Audit Team of the Municipality the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund on or before the 15th day after the end of each month through the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council and the Municipal Development Council.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive through the Sangguniang Bayan hire qualified personnel to occupy the position of LDRRM Officer and three (3) Staff following the position titles and salary grades set by Section 12 of RA No. 10121 and Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 so that performance of functions and responsibilities as LDRRM Officer and Staff could be carried out effectively especially in times of disaster and or calamity.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive work/program for the provision of a garage where all of the vehicles should be kept when not in use to preserve them from early deterioration. Provisions of Section 361 (e) of GAAM, Vol. 1, should be strictly followed to maximize the use of motor vehicles and to avoid incurrence of additional expense for their maintenance.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive create a Disposal Committee to take charge of the disposal of unserviceable properties and equipment. 

We also recommend that the said Committee will be guided by the procedures and guidelines on the disposal as well as the forms to be used in reporting thereof.

We recommend that the Supply Officer should issue Acknowledgment Receipt of Equipment on property and equipment purchased pursuant to Section 492 of GAAM, Vol. 1.  It should be in the name of the official who is in actual possession of the property to pinpoint responsibility and accountability.  The person entrusted with its custody and control shall be responsible for its proper use and care and shall exercise due diligence in the utilization and safekeeping thereof.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the previous Municipal Treasurer to liquidate immediately all her cash advances pursuant to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-004.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor should require the Treasurer to pay the bank service charge and take extra careful in handling government funds. Prepare the necessary adjusting entry for the bank service charge to Account Receivable to both of them.
